description: Figure 1 presents the phase evolution and microstructural characterization of a cold-rolled (CR) Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex lightweight steel after annealing at 600–1050 °C for 5 min. Panel (a) shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns revealing that the CR state consists of austenite (FCC, red diamonds) and ferrite (BCC, blue diamonds). As annealing temperature increases, a β phase (brown diamonds) emerges at 700–900 °C, followed by Cr₇C₃ carbides (orange diamonds) and κ-carbides (green diamonds) at 950–1000 °C; these carbides dissolve significantly above 1000 °C, yielding a duplex FCC+BCC structure at 1050 °C. Panel (b) presents TEM bright-field images, EDS mapping, and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED, B=[0001]) of sample DP950–5, identifying Cr₇C₃ carbides precipitated at grain boundaries with sizes of hundreds of nanometers. Panel (c) shows TEM micrographs and SAED patterns of DP1000–5, distinguishing ferrite (zone 2, B=[023]) from austenite containing intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles (zone 3, B=[011]); high-resolution TEM quantifies these nanoparticles as 0.86 ± 0.20 nm in radius with a volume fraction of 1.56 %. Collectively, the data demonstrate that annealing at 1000 °C for 5 min produces a fully recrystallized austenite-ferrite duplex matrix strengthened by ultrafine intragranular κ-carbides rather than brittle intergranular carbides, underpinning the observed strength-ductility synergy.
